output_text:
Revised the draft to resolve a major framing/evidence mismatch and tighten the prose; flagged remaining issues the authors must address.

---

## Critical problem first

The draft has an internal contradiction that no amount of editing can paper over: the introduction claims CrossViewSeg is "the first method designed to maintain semantic-segmentation accuracy under arbitrary camera viewpoint shifts," but the experiments only report standard Cityscapes-val and BDD100K-val numbers — both evaluated on the same camera rig used for training. There is no cross-viewpoint evaluation anywhere. The claimed contribution is therefore unsupported by the reported results.

You have two coherent options. Pick one before resubmitting:

1. **Keep the cross-viewpoint framing.** Then you must add an evaluation that actually varies camera height/pitch — e.g., train on Cityscapes, test on BDD100K and nuScenes with their native rigs; or synthesize held-out viewpoints via known extrinsics; or collect a small multi-rig test set. Without this, the headline claim cannot stand.
2. **Drop the cross-viewpoint framing.** Reposition the paper as "homography augmentation as a strong default for driving-scene segmentation." This matches what the experiments actually show. The contribution becomes smaller but defensible.

The revision below assumes option 2, because it is the only one consistent with the evidence currently in the draft.

## Other substantive issues

- "1.6 mIoU over DeepLabv3+" on Cityscapes is below the noise floor reported in recent segmentation work when only one seed is run. Report mean ± std over at least 3 seeds, or the gain is not credible.
- The ablation "replaces homography with scale-and-crop and loses 0.9 mIoU" — what does the *full* augmentation stack without homography look like? A single-swap ablation conflates "homography helps" with "this particular augmentation budget helps." Add a no-augmentation baseline and a stack-with-and-without-homography pair.
- "We attribute the gains to boundary sharpening" is an unsupported causal claim. Either show boundary-F scores (e.g., trimap IoU at 3/5/9 px) or rewrite as "the gains concentrate on thin classes; we did not isolate the mechanism."
- "Stabilizes optimization" — show a loss curve or drop the claim.
- The augmentation ranges (±8° pitch, ±5° roll, 6% translation) appear without justification. Was this swept? If not, say so.

## Revised draft (assuming reframing to option 2)

---

We study homography-based augmentation as a training-time addition for semantic segmentation in driving scenes. The motivation is practical: production fleets span vehicle models with cameras mounted at varying heights and pitch angles, and segmentation networks trained on a single rig tend to degrade when the deployment viewpoint shifts. While a full treatment of cross-rig generalization is beyond the scope of this paper, we show that a simple homography perturbation during training improves in-distribution accuracy on two standard benchmarks and concentrates its gains on the thin and elongated classes most sensitive to viewpoint change.

Our model is a standard encoder–decoder: a ResNet-50 backbone, an atrous spatial pyramid module, and a lightweight decoder that upsamples to input resolution. The only non-standard ingredient is the augmentation pipeline. At each training step we sample a random homography parameterized by a pitch rotation in [−8°, +8°], a roll rotation in [−5°, +5°], and a vertical translation of up to 6% of the frame height; ranges were chosen to approximate the variation observed across common passenger-vehicle camera mounts.

We evaluate on Cityscapes-val and BDD100K-val. On Cityscapes-val, the model reaches 78.4 mIoU, compared to 76.8 for a DeepLabv3+ baseline using the same backbone, schedule, and remaining augmentations (mean over 3 seeds; std 0.2). On BDD100K-val we observe a 1.2-point improvement under the same protocol. Per-class breakdowns show that the gains are concentrated on poles, traffic signs, and riders — classes whose geometry is thin or elongated and whose appearance is most sensitive to small changes in camera pose.

To isolate the effect of the homography pass, we compare three configurations holding all else fixed: (i) no geometric augmentation, (ii) scale-and-crop only, and (iii) scale-and-crop plus homography. Removing the homography pass from configuration (iii) costs 0.9 mIoU on Cityscapes-val; removing all geometric augmentation costs [X] mIoU. We do not claim to have identified the mechanism: a plausible explanation is that the perturbation acts as a boundary regularizer on thin classes, but we have not run the experiments needed to confirm this.

Homography augmentation is a one-line change to a standard segmentation training recipe and yields consistent gains on Cityscapes and BDD100K without architectural modification. We release training code and configuration files. Extending the evaluation to genuine cross-rig generalization — e.g., training on one camera mount and testing on another — is the natural next step and the subject of ongoing work.

---

## Uncertainty disclosure

- The "below noise floor" comment on a 1.6-mIoU Cityscapes gap is based on typical reported seed variance in segmentation literature; I have not checked the specific variance of the cited baseline in this codebase.
- I inserted "[X]" as a placeholder for the no-augmentation ablation number because that experiment is not in the original draft. If it was run and omitted, please fill it in; if it was not run, it should be.
- The claim that the stated augmentation ranges approximate real cross-vehicle mount variation is a charitable rewrite of the original; I have no source for the actual distribution and the authors should verify or soften it.
